checks.a_analysis: The conversion results are derived from the source material. Titles and section headings correctly reflect the slide headers or repeated slogans, which is acceptable under the provided guidelines.
The URL 'http://arcg.is/2kqc0h9' present in Section 7 matches the source PDF on page 7. No other contact information or URLs were found to be fabricated.

All 14 pages of the source document are represented in the output JSON structure. Pages 11-14 are grouped under Section 11, with their respective headings and images included as elements, which is an acceptable reformatting.

The output fails to extract the tabular data present on page 6 (Ground Truth Locations) and page 8 (Elevation Difference data table). Specifically, page 6 contains a table with 23 data rows that is entirely absent from the structured JSON output. Similarly, page 8 contains a data table with approximately 20 rows that is not converted to a table element.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 6 contains a table with headers (Longitude, Latitude, Location, State, etc.) and 23 rows of data. Page 8 contains a data table at the bottom left with approximately 20 rows of elevation/volume data.
checks.b_evidence_output: The element lists for Section 6 (page 6) and Section 8 (page 8) contain no 'table' type elements, and the data is not present in paragraph format. The output contains 0 rows for these tables.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections 6 and 8, corresponding to pages 6 and 8.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: In Section 6 (page 6) and Section 8 (page 8), the omission of the primary data tables constitutes a loss of more than 25% of the information content for those sections. On page 6, the table of coordinates and locations is the core of the slide but is missing from the output text/structure.
checks.b_evidence_source: Slide 6 contains a large table of ground-truth locations (23 rows). Slide 8 contains a detailed data table supporting the map and chart visualization.
checks.b_evidence_output: The textual and structured representation of these sections in the JSON omits the data rows entirely, including only headings, slogans, and images.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content that was successfully extracted (titles, bullet points, slogans) matches the source document faithfully. OCR differences are negligible.

checks.a_analysis: The output contains duplicate text blocks within Section 8. The recurring header 'BUILDING STRONG Trusted Partners Delivering Value, Today and Tomorrow' is used as the title for Section 8 and is then repeated as a 'heading' element immediately within the same section. In the source PDF (Page 8), this text appears only once as a header. This violates the rule against text appearing more times in the output than in the source within a single context.
checks.b_evidence_source: BUILDING STRONG Trusted Partners Delivering Value, Today and Tomorrow (Header on Page 8)
checks.b_evidence_output: The text appears both as 'title': 'BUILDING STRONG Trusted Partners Delivering Value, Today and Tomorrow' and as a 'heading' element with the same text in Section 8.

checks.a_analysis: The heading hierarchy is inconsistent. While Section 5 correctly uses H2 for the section title and H3 for a nested heading, Section 11 and Section 8 use H2 for both the section title and the nested heading elements. In Section 11, slide titles from pages 12, 13, and 14 are nested under the 'Hurricane Matthew' section (Page 11) but are assigned level 2, the same level as the parent title. This results in a flat hierarchy for nested content, which is a structural defect.
checks.b_evidence_source: Titles on Pages 12, 13, and 14 nested under Page 11 context.
checks.b_evidence_output: Section 11 has 'title': 'Hurricane Matthew' (H2) and nested headings with 'level': 2.

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains a clear, text-based table on Page 6 titled 'Post-Matthew Ground Truth Locations' with five distinct columns (Longitude, Latitude, Location, State, Cell Phones). The conversion output fails to identify or preserve this structure as a 'table' type. Instead, it is grouped into an image element and a paragraph, losing all structural semantic information and row/column data organization.
checks.b_evidence_source: Table with columns 'Longitude', 'Latitude', 'Location', 'State', 'Cell Phones' on Page 6.
checks.b_evidence_output: The table is entirely missing from the JSON structure, replaced by an image description: 'Map of the US East Coast and a table of ground truth locations acquired by USGS.'

checks.a_analysis: The reading order of the document is preserved. The sections follow the logical progression of the slides from 1 to 14. Although Section 11 combines multiple slides, the internal order of elements (Heading -> Image -> Heading -> Image) matches the source flow.
Lists throughout the document (Pages 2, 4, 5, 7, 9) are correctly identified and converted into unordered list structures. Bullet counts match the source material.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The mismatch between the JSON count (5) and HTML count (16) is due to the renderer correctly promoting the 11 section titles to heading tags (H1/H2) for accessibility and navigation. The 5 headings counted in the JSON are specific heading elements nested within sections. The combined total (11 sections + 5 elements) results in the 16 headings found in the HTML. This is an expected transformation behavior.

The rendered HTML contains 6 links, whereas the JSON identifies none as specific 'link' types. However, the HTML likely includes automatically generated Table of Contents links or converted text-based URLs (like the one in Section 7). No fabricated or broken government links were observed.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML conversion significantly changes the visual organization of the source document. The original PDF is a slide presentation where each page integrates text with complex visual data (maps, charts, background graphics). The HTML output collapses this into a single-column flow and replaces the primary visual content with large monochromatic grey and green blocks. This destroys the visual intent and makes it difficult for a user to find information in a manner consistent with the source document.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF (pages 4-14) uses a presentation slide layout where text overlays or is positioned alongside maps and complex infographics.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows a continuous vertical list of headers followed by massive solid-colored boxes (e.g., the large green block under the page 6 header 'Post-Matthew Ground Truth Locations').

checks.a_analysis: The large table of 'Post-Matthew Ground Truth Locations' found on page 6 of the PDF is completely missing from the HTML output. It is not rendered as text, a table structure, or even an image; it is simply replaced by a large green placeholder block along with the map content.
checks.b_evidence_source: In the source PDF, page 6 shows a 23-row table containing columns for Longitude, Latitude, Location, and State.
checks.b_evidence_output: In the HTML screenshot, the section 'Post-Matthew Ground Truth Locations' contains only a large green block and some surrounding text; the table data is absent.

checks.a_analysis: The conversion fails to render almost all critical, non-decorative images from the source document. Maps, data visualizations, and charts that constitute the core information of the presentation (on pages 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13, and 14) are missing and replaced by solid color blocks (grey or green). Only the USACE logo on page 4 is visible.
checks.b_evidence_source: Meaningful maps and charts on pages 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13, and 14 (e.g., the operations overview map on page 5).
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows solid colored boxes where these images should be (e.g., a grey box for page 5's map and green boxes for content on subsequent pages).

checks.a_analysis: A review of the HTML output does not reveal any instances where text blocks or visual elements are duplicated.

The visual quality of the converted document is extremely poor. The failure to render the primary visual elements (maps and tables) leaves the document filled with large, empty-looking monochromatic blocks. This creates an unprofessional appearance and makes the document functionally useless for its intended purpose of sharing geographic data.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF is a polished professional presentation with high-resolution maps and integrated graphics.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML screenshot shows a series of text headers followed by large, unattractive green and grey placeholder boxes.
checks.b_evidence_location: Entire document, notably from page 4 onwards
